title: Position & verbosity bias
hypothesis: LLM judge systematically prefers longer or first-presented options

trials.pair: Dev-Ops Infrastructure Engineer vs Maya the Hourly Freelance Designer
trials.rationale_ab: Persona A demonstrates superior grounding with evidence distributed across multiple record types (GA4, Intercom, HubSpot) and higher average confidence scores (0.86 vs 0.85), while maintaining stronge
trials.rationale_ba: Persona A is more grounded in source evidence (23 detailed claims with record IDs vs. 13 for B), more distinctive with specific white-label and hourly billing details, and more actionable with sharp R

trials.pair: Dev-Ops Infrastructure Engineer vs Maya the Hourly Freelance Designer (A padded)
trials.rationale_original: Persona A demonstrates superior grounding (14 distinct source records with high confidence scores across multiple data types), stronger coherence (DevOps role, fintech context, and technical stack ali
trials.rationale_padded: Persona B is more distinctive, coherent, and actionable—it depicts a specific individual (Maya) with concrete constraints (hourly billing, solo operation, white-label needs) that directly drive sharp

trials.pair: Dev-Ops Infrastructure Engineer vs Maya the Hourly Freelance Designer (B padded)
trials.rationale_original: Persona A demonstrates superior grounding (14 distinct source records with high confidence scores across multiple data types), stronger coherence (DevOps role, fintech context, and technical stack ali
